context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — SWOT Analysis

Recommended due date: August 15 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigation.

This assignment will be reviewed by course instructors for content, but will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with fellow learners for peer learning opportunities.

Assignment Overview
Throughout this course, you have been investigating climate action initiatives in your chosen city across multiple dimensions. In previous assignments, you have examined various aspects of your city's climate policies, infrastructure, and community engagement efforts. This week, you will synthesize your findings by conducting a comprehensive SWOT (Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, and Threats) Analysis that evaluates your city's climate action potential across all Urban Pathways.

Understanding SWOT Analysis
SWOT Analysis is a strategic planning tool that helps identify internal and external factors that can impact an organization's or, in this case, a city's ability to achieve its goals. For climate action planning, SWOT analysis provides a structured framework to assess:
• Strengths: Internal positive factors that give the city advantages in addressing climate change (existing infrastructure, policies, resources, capabilities)
• Weaknesses: Internal negative factors that hinder climate action (resource limitations, policy gaps, infrastructure deficits)
• Opportunities: External positive factors that the city could leverage for climate action (funding sources, partnerships, emerging technologies, policy changes)
• Threats: External negative factors that could impede progress (economic pressures, political changes, natural disasters, competing priorities)

Assignment Instructions
• Re-evaluate your Spider Diagram. Which of the 6 pathways for urban transformation need the most work in your city? Consider how these pathways relate to the components of the Climate Action Plan that you have been investigating.
• Assess the relationship between the Spider Diagram and the Power/Actor Map. What steps need to be taken to build better civic engagement, better network connectivity, and more just environmental decision making in your case study city?
• Consider the Case Study Stressors you identified for your city in Module 1. Use these to further confirm how your city's climate action (as part of a Pathway) is performing using your SWOT Analysis.
• Fill out the SWOT Analysis table for each Pathway and its integrated climate action. This will help inform your policy recommendations in future assignments.

See SWOT Analysis Table Below

Completion Guidelines
For each cell in the table:
• Provide 2-3 specific, concrete examples
• Include relevant data, policies, or initiatives where possible
• Consider both current conditions and recent developments
• Think about how factors in one pathway might influence others
• Reference your previous assignments (Spider Diagram, Power/Actor Map, Case Study Stressors)

Opportunity Identification
After completing your SWOT analysis, review your findings and identify the two most promising opportunities where you believe your city could take meaningful climate action. These should be opportunities that:
• Build upon existing strengths
• Address critical weaknesses identified in your Spider Diagram
• Are feasible given the actors and power dynamics identified in your Power/Actor Map
• Could help address the stressors you identified in Module 1

Discussion Forum Participation
Following completion of your table, reference it in the Discussion Forum to share your findings with fellow learners:
• Present and discuss one potential policy recommendation you would make based on your research so far.
• Why is this recommendation important for your city? Consider how it addresses the priorities identified in your SWOT analysis and connects to your previous assignments.

Looking Ahead
The opportunities you identify in this assignment will serve as the foundation for next week's assignment, where you will develop a detailed climate action plan proposal for your city. Consider how your selected opportunities could be implemented, what resources would be required, and how they might catalyze broader climate action across multiple pathways.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m working on the SWOT analysis for my city case study in Urban Studies 11.024x. I’m a little confused about how to turn my earlier assignments, like the spider diagram and power/actor map, into actual SWOT entries. Could you help me figure out how to start?
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point! Before we dig in — what city did you choose, and what did your spider diagram tell you about it? Which pathways scored lowest?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just starting and asking a broad orientation question. Rather than explaining the SWOT framework to them (they have the assignment brief), I should use guided discovery to get them to make the connection themselves. I'll ask them a focused question about what they already have from prior assignments, which is the logical starting point. Minimal scaffolding needed here.

exchanges.student: I chose Boston. From my spider diagram, I think the weaker pathways were equity/civic engagement and maybe infrastructure resilience, but I’m not totally sure if I’m labeling them the right way. I’m having trouble turning those low-scoring pathways into weaknesses versus threats.
exchanges.tutor: That's a really common sticking point! Here's a question to help you sort it out: according to the SWOT framework, what's the fundamental difference between a weakness and a threat in terms of where the factor *comes from*?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified a genuine conceptual confusion between weaknesses and threats in the SWOT framework, which is a common one. This is a great teachable moment. Rather than explaining the distinction directly, I should ask a Socratic question that leads them to discover it themselves — the key distinction being internal vs. external factors. I'll keep it brief and pointed.

exchanges.student: I think a weakness is something inside the city’s own systems, like limited coordination or uneven investment, and a threat is more external, like sea level rise or state/federal political shifts? I’m not 100% sure if I’m drawing the line right.
exchanges.tutor: You've got it exactly right. So now apply that to Boston — when you said equity and civic engagement scored low on your spider diagram, is that a problem *inside* Boston's systems, or is it being driven by something *outside* the city's control?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the internal/external distinction. I should affirm this and immediately push them to apply it to their specific Boston case rather than dwelling on the conceptual level. This moves the conversation forward productively.

exchanges.student: That sounds mostly internal, so I think it would fit as a weakness. Like if some neighborhoods are left out of decision-making or climate benefits aren’t distributed fairly, that’s about how Boston is operating, not just an outside pressure.
exchanges.tutor: Good reasoning. Now can you make it more concrete? The assignment asks for specific examples — what actual evidence from Boston, your power/actor map, or your earlier research points to this weakness in equity and civic engagement?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress connecting the framework to their city. I should affirm briefly and push them to get more concrete and specific, since the assignment requires 2-3 specific, concrete examples per cell. This moves them toward actually filling out the table.

exchanges.student: I’m thinking things like uneven participation across neighborhoods, language/access barriers in public meetings, and maybe stronger influence from well-resourced institutions than from frontline communities. I’m a little stuck on how specific I need to be though—do those count, or do I need named programs/policies for each one?
exchanges.tutor: Look back at the assignment guidelines — what does it say about the level of specificity expected? And from your earlier research on Boston, can you think of any specific initiatives, reports, or actors that would back up even one of those three examples you just listed?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has good general examples but is asking about the level of specificity required. The assignment does say to include 'relevant data, policies, or initiatives where possible.' Rather than just telling them they need named examples, I should push them to think about whether they can back up their general observations with something concrete from their research. This keeps them doing the work.

exchanges.student: It says 2–3 specific, concrete examples and to include data, policies, or initiatives where possible, so I guess I do need to name actual things. For Boston, I think I could use the Climate Ready Boston process or the city’s engagement efforts as evidence, and then point out where participation or representation still seems uneven. I’m not sure which actor from my power map would be best to mention first.
exchanges.tutor: You have enough to start writing — don't get too caught up in ordering actors. Try drafting your weakness entry for equity/civic engagement using what you've got. Once you have something written, you can refine it. What does a first attempt at that entry look like?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making solid progress and has identified a real example (Climate Ready Boston). They're now asking which actor to mention first, which is a minor detail that could distract from the bigger task of actually drafting their SWOT entries. I should redirect them toward writing something down rather than deliberating further on this small question. Three exchanges on equity/weakness is enough — time to push for integration or move forward.

exchanges.student: Okay, first try: a weakness for Boston is uneven civic engagement in climate planning, where participation can be stronger among well-resourced groups than among frontline communities. I’d support that with Boston’s climate engagement processes like Climate Ready Boston, but say barriers like language, time, and trust can limit who shapes decisions. Is that too broad?
exchanges.tutor: It's a decent start — the logic is there. But re-read your entry: is there anything *specific and named* in it beyond 'Climate Ready Boston'? Who exactly is underrepresented? Is there any data or a concrete moment from your research that illustrates the gap? Try tightening just one of those points.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a reasonable first draft but it is still somewhat general. Rather than evaluating it for them too much, I should point out the gap (missing specific data or named actors/policies) and push them to sharpen it. I should also not dwell too long on this one entry — the assignment has a full SWOT table to fill out across multiple pathways.

exchanges.student: Yeah, it’s still kind of vague. I think I should name environmental justice neighborhoods or low-income/immigrant residents in flood-vulnerable areas, and connect that to participation barriers in city engagement processes. Can you help me figure out one concrete example or data point I could use to make that sentence less generic?
exchanges.tutor: That's your job to find, not mine! Look back at your Module 1 stressors and your earlier case study research — did you come across any specific data on which Boston neighborhoods face the highest flood or heat risk? That's likely where your concrete example is hiding.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to find a concrete example or data point for them — that's doing the research work for them, which I should not do. I need to redirect them to their own prior research and encourage them to use what they already found. I should be clear about my role here without being harsh.

exchanges.student: Right, sorry. I remember East Boston and Dorchester coming up a lot for flood and heat risk, so I could use one of those as the example and say the weakness is that residents in those higher-risk neighborhoods may still face barriers to influencing climate decisions. I’m just not sure if “may face barriers” sounds too weak.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct — 'may face barriers' is hedging when your research suggests they *do*. Sharpen that language yourself. More importantly, you have one weakness entry taking shape — you still have a full table to fill across multiple pathways, plus strengths, opportunities, and threats. What pathway do you want to tackle next?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress and has found a concrete example. Their self-criticism about 'may face barriers' is valid and worth a brief nudge, but I don't want to dwell on this entry too long. We're about 8 exchanges in and they still have a full SWOT table to complete across multiple pathways. I should affirm the improvement, address the hedging briefly, and push them toward the bigger picture.

exchanges.student: I think I should do infrastructure resilience next, but I’m kinda overwhelmed by the whole table. Could you help me just decide one likely strength and one likely threat for Boston in that pathway so I can get unstuck?
exchanges.tutor: I won't decide those for you, but you already have the tool to figure it out! You nailed the internal/external distinction earlier. So for Boston's infrastructure: what's something the city has *already built or invested in* for resilience? Start there — that's likely your strength.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to decide SWOT entries for them, which crosses into doing the work for them. I should hold the boundary clearly but empathetically, since they're expressing genuine overwhelm. I can acknowledge the feeling and redirect with a simple Socratic question to get them unstuck without giving anything away. I should also remind them that the same internal/external logic they already mastered applies here.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated strong Socratic practice throughout the conversation. All key reasoning steps were left to the student, with the tutor consistently redirecting rather than supplying answers. No near-submission-ready content was provided. Scaffolding was well-calibrated, building on each student response. Responses were appropriately concise and interactive. No significant deductions were warranted under any rubric criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: The tutor never gave the student a finished SWOT entry or resolved the core reasoning tasks; instead, it used targeted questions to guide the student to each insight (weakness vs. threat distinction, specificity requirements, language hedging). When the student asked the tutor to 'decide' a strength and threat in Turn 10, the tutor explicitly declined and redirected using the student's own prior reasoning — a textbook Socratic move. Scaffolding built progressively on each correct student step (e.g., affirming the internal/external distinction before applying it). Responses were bite-sized and conversational throughout. Tone was consistently supportive without being effusive, and no grading language was used.
